turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: I've just had this persistent cough for a while now. It started out dry and then it turned into this yucky yellowish-green stuff coming up.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: It's been driving me crazy, to be honest. The cough suppressant hasn't even touched it, so I'm worried something more is going on.

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: I've been feeling really tired and fatigued, even when I try to push through my work as a teacher. And sometimes, especially at night, I get this low-grade fever that's been around 38.2 degrees Celsius. It's not too bad, but it's definitely there.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: Actually, yes. I've been having night sweats pretty badly. Every night for the past 4 nights, I've woken up with my pajamas soaked through. It's really worrying me.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: It mainly stays around my chest, but sometimes it feels like it's spreading to my throat and even into my lungs when I cough. But it's always the worst in my chest area.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient: To be honest, just having this persistent cough is really getting to me, and now you're mentioning that it might spread... it makes me worried about what could be going on inside my body. Sometimes I feel a bit short of breath when I'm exerting myself or even just walking upstairs, but I've been trying to brush it off as just being out of shape.

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't really paid much attention to swelling, but my throat can feel a bit sore and tight when I swallow after coughing.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't really thought about feeling nauseous or having stomach issues, but I have noticed that my appetite has been really off lately. I've had trouble eating and just don't feel like cooking or trying new foods, even though I usually love to cook for myself and my partner.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: To be honest, my energy levels have been really low lately. I've missed 3 days of work already because I just couldn't get out of bed or face another day of teaching with this cough and fatigue weighing me down. And like you mentioned, my appetite has been pretty much gone for the past 5 days - it's hard to even think about eating when all I can focus on is trying to breathe and feel better.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: I've already told you about my low-grade fever. It's been around 38.2 degrees Celsius, and it's worse in the evenings. And like I mentioned earlier, I've also had night sweats every night for the past 4 nights. That's really been the most concerning part for me - the sweating at night has just been overwhelming.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient: I have seasonal allergies, and I take over-the-counter cetirizine when my symptoms get bad. I also had a penicillin allergy test done years ago because of a rash reaction to it, but that's about it.

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been trying to take my cetirizine as needed for my allergies, but with this cough and fatigue going on, it's been tough to remember to take them. And as for the dextromethorphan cough suppressant, I've just been taking it every 4-6 hours like the package says, but it really hasn't helped much, so I'm worried there's something more going on that it can't touch.

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: Honestly, with my appetite being off like it has been for the past 5 days, I've just been grabbing whatever is easy and quick to make or eating light meals. Usually, I'm more of a home cook and enjoy trying out new recipes, but lately, even thinking about cooking feels overwhelming.

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been trying to keep up with my usual routine, but it's hard when I'm feeling this tired and fatigued all the time. As a teacher, I normally try to walk to school or take the bus, but lately, even walking upstairs has left me short of breath, so I've had to cut back on physical activity.

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: My mom has asthma. She's always been pretty open about managing it with her inhalers and whatnot. It used to be a big concern for me when I was younger, but now that I'm older, I've learned more about how different asthma can be from person to person. Still, hearing you ask this makes me wonder if there might be some connection...

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: Yes, it's definitely made me more aware and worried about my own health, especially with this persistent cough and all the other symptoms I've been experiencing. It's hard not to think about the possibility that something might be seriously wrong when you see a family member dealing with chronic conditions like asthma.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: I have a glass of wine on weekends, maybe one or two glasses, but that's about it. As for coping with stress, I usually try to talk things over with my partner or friends, and we'll often go out and do something together to take our minds off things. Lately, though, even those usual ways of dealing with stress haven't been as effective because I've just felt so overwhelmed by this persistent cough and all the other symptoms.

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: No, I don't smoke at all. And to be honest, with everything that's been going on, I haven't really considered using substances to cope, my mind has just been focused on trying to figure out what's causing these symptoms and how to get rid of them.

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: I've been worried about mentioning my seasonal allergies, but I think it might be relevant since we were talking about asthma and family history. And to be honest, I have been feeling a bit anxious about sharing all this with you because I'm really scared about what could be going on inside my body. But I want to be open and honest, so please keep reassuring me that everything is okay.

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient: I've been trying not to think about it, but I guess it's relevant - my partner and some of my students have had upper respiratory infections recently. It might seem unrelated, but I couldn't help but wonder if maybe something was going around that could be connected to what I'm experiencing.
